Recent research emphasizes systematic evaluation and architectural design of AI agents across diverse domains, with key findings that agent architecture can shift performance by twelve percentage points while backbone models remain dominant, and that production systems require workflow graphs balancing flexibility with strict constraints rather than pure autonomy.

Copied!
research relevance 0.92

General Agent Evaluation

arxiv.org· Bandel, Elron, Yehudai, Asaf, Eden, Lilach, Sagron, Yehoshua, Perlitz, Yotam, Venezian, Elad, Razinkov, Natalia, Ergas, Natan, Ifergan, Shlomit Shachor, Shlomov, Segev, Jacovi, Michal, Choshen, Leshem, Ein-Dor, Liat, Katz, Yoav, Shmueli-Scheuer, Michal · 2026-05-24 (discovered)

This paper presents the first systematic evaluation of general-purpose AI agents across diverse environments and benchmarks. The authors develop a unified protocol and evaluation harness enabling fair comparison of tool-calling, MCP, code-generation, and CLI agent architectures using identical backbone models across six benchmarks spanning software engineering, customer service, research, and personal assistance. Key findings show that agent architecture can swing performance by 12 percentage points within a single model, yet backbone model choice dominates overall results. Notably, general agents achieve performance comparable to heavily-customized domain-specific agents on 4 of 6 benchmarks, while open-weight models exhibit unexpected failure modes absent from frontier closed-source models. This work provides critical infrastructure and insights for understanding how architectural choices impact agent generalization across domains.

Key takeaways
  • General agents successfully adapt to diverse domains without per-domain customization across six distinct benchmarks.
  • Agent architecture choice swings performance by up to 12pp, but backbone model dominance outweighs architectural differences.
  • Open-weight models exhibit domain-specific "generality sinks" where they collapse on particular architectures or benchmarks.
  • Top general agents match heavily-customized domain-specific agents on 4 of 6 tested benchmarks.
  • Behavioral failure analysis reveals architecture-distinctive error patterns that aggregate scoring masks.
#agent evaluation#AI systems#benchmark methodology
Read article →
research relevance 0.88

A Practical Approach for Building Production-Grade Conversational Agents with Workflow Graphs

arxiv.org· Park, Chiwan, Jang, Wonjun, Kim, Daeryong, Ahn, Aelim, Yang, Kichang, Hwang, Woosung, Roh, Jihyeon, Park, Hyerin, Wang, Hyosun, Kim, Min Seok, Kang, Jihoon · 2026-05-24 (discovered)

This paper addresses the challenge of deploying large language models in production conversational agents, specifically for e-commerce. The authors propose a workflow graph approach that balances the flexibility of LLMs with strict service-specific constraints—a tension arising from LLMs' probabilistic nature. They present a detailed case study of their implementation, strategies for overcoming real-world limitations, and a framework for building scalable, controllable, and reliable AI agents. The work bridges the gap between academic research and industrial deployment, offering practical insights valuable to engineers building production-grade conversational systems.

Key takeaways
  • Workflow graphs enable controllable LLM-based agents by constraining probabilistic outputs while maintaining conversational flexibility in production settings.
  • Real-world deployment requires balancing SOTA research capabilities with strict compliance and service-specific constraints inherent to industrial applications.
  • E-commerce case study demonstrates practical optimization strategies and implementation patterns for scalable, reliable conversational AI systems.
  • Framework addresses core tension: LLMs' stochastic nature conflicts with deterministic service requirements in production environments.
#conversational agents#LLM production systems#workflow graphs
Read article →
opinion relevance 0.85

Humans and Agents in Software Engineering Loops

martinfowler.com· Kief Morris · 2026-05-24 (discovered)

Kief Morris explores the optimal positioning of humans in AI-assisted software development, proposing a framework between two extremes: humans completely outside the 'how loop' (vibe coding) or micromanaging every decision. Morris advocates for humans to be 'on the loop'—managing feedback cycles and setting quality standards rather than inspecting individual code changes. He distinguishes between the 'why loop' (iterating on ideas and goals) and nested 'how loops' (implementation details), arguing that while external quality (functionality, performance, compliance) matters, internal quality (code cleanliness) should be managed through design standards and feedback loops rather than line-by-line review. The article challenges the assumption that LLMs don't benefit from well-structured codebases and proposes humans should focus on steering outcomes through strategic feedback rather than tactical code review.

Key takeaways
  • Humans should manage feedback loops and quality standards ('on the loop'), not micromanage code generation or abandon oversight entirely
  • Internal code quality improves LLM productivity and reduces spiraling; it's not merely aesthetic but affects external outcomes through development speed and cost
  • Focus on external quality (functionality, performance, compliance) and use design practices like ADRs and tests as quality gates, not human code inspection bottlenecks
  • The 'why loop' (goals/ideas) is fundamentally human-driven; the 'how loop' (implementation) benefits from agent-human collaboration at the right abstraction levels
#AI agents#software development#human-AI collaboration
Read article →
research relevance 0.82

Measuring Agent Autonomy

anthropic.com· Unknown author · 2026-05-24 (discovered)

This article examines frameworks for quantifying agent autonomy in AI systems, establishing metrics to assess how independently agents operate without human oversight. It addresses the challenge of defining and measuring autonomy across different agent architectures and deployment contexts. Technical readers should care because autonomy metrics are critical for evaluating production-ready agentic systems, enabling better risk assessment, governance decisions, and comparative evaluation across different agent designs and implementations.

Key takeaways
  • Autonomy metrics must account for decision complexity, intervention frequency, and outcome quality across diverse task domains.
  • Clear autonomy definitions prevent conflation with capability or reliability—an agent can be capable but low-autonomy or unreliable despite high autonomy.
  • Multi-dimensional measurement frameworks outperform single-number autonomy scores for practical governance and deployment decisions.
  • Human-in-the-loop checkpoints and fallback mechanisms quantifiably reduce measured autonomy but improve safety and trust.
#agent autonomy#agentic AI#evaluation metrics
Read article →
research relevance 0.75

Agent Evals

developers.openai.com· Unknown author · 2026-05-24 (discovered)

This article examines methodologies for evaluating AI agents, addressing the challenge of assessing agent performance beyond traditional metrics. The piece argues that effective agent evaluation requires multi-dimensional approaches that consider goal completion, efficiency, safety, and interpretability. Technical readers should care because proper evaluation frameworks are critical for validating agent systems in production, enabling comparison of different architectures, and identifying failure modes before deployment. The article likely explores concrete evaluation techniques applicable to various agent types and use cases.

Key takeaways
  • Multi-dimensional evaluation metrics essential—single metrics miss critical agent behaviors and failure modes
  • Safety and interpretability assessment must be prioritized alongside task completion rates
  • Evaluation frameworks should account for efficiency, cost, and resource usage in agent systems
  • Standardized benchmarks enable meaningful comparison across different agent architectures
  • Real-world testing required—simulated environments often fail to capture complex agent behaviors
#agent evaluation#AI systems#benchmarking
Read article →
tutorial relevance 0.72

Evaluation Best Practices

developers.openai.com· Unknown author · 2026-05-24 (discovered)

This article outlines best practices for evaluating agents, emphasizing the importance of structured evaluation methodologies. The piece covers key principles for designing robust evaluation frameworks, including establishing clear metrics, defining test datasets, and implementing systematic assessment procedures. Technical teams building and deploying agent systems should care because rigorous evaluation directly impacts reliability, safety, and performance in production environments.

Key takeaways
  • Define measurable metrics aligned with specific agent goals before evaluation begins
  • Use diverse, representative test datasets to assess generalization and edge cases
  • Implement systematic benchmarking procedures to enable reproducible comparisons across iterations
  • Document evaluation methodology for transparency and future auditing
#agent evaluation#best practices#testing

research relevance 0.88

Position: Agentic Evolution is the Path to Evolving LLMs

arxiv.org· Lin, Minhua, Lu, Hanqing, Shi, Zhan, He, Bing, Mao, Rui, Zhang, Zhiwei, Wu, Zongyu, Tang, Xianfeng, Liu, Hui, Dai, Zhenwei, Zhang, Xiang, Wang, Suhang, Dumoulin, Benoit, Pei, Jian · 2026-05-24 (discovered)

This position paper argues that agentic evolution represents a fundamental new scaling axis for adapting Large Language Models beyond static training. The authors identify a critical limitation: as LLMs deploy into dynamic real-world environments, static training cannot keep pace with continual change. While scaling compute at training or inference time improves baseline capabilities, it fails to close the train-deploy gap. The paper proposes A-Evolve, a framework treating deployment-time improvement as goal-directed optimization over persistent system state, where an autonomous agent diagnoses failures and produces durable improvements. Central to their thesis is the evolution-scaling hypothesis: adaptation capacity scales with compute allocated to evolution, positioning agentic evolution as a scalable path toward sustained, open-ended LLM adaptation.

Key takeaways
  • Static training cannot keep pace with deployment environment change; evolution becomes necessary scaling axis
  • Existing fine-tuning and memory methods lack strategic agency to diagnose failures and produce durable improvements
  • A-Evolve framework treats deployment-time adaptation as deliberate optimization over persistent system state
  • Evolution-scaling hypothesis: adaptation capacity scales with compute allocated to evolution
  • Agentic evolution enables sustained, open-ended adaptation in real-world deployment environments
#LLM adaptation#agentic systems#deployment optimization
Read article →
research relevance 0.88

Towards More Standardized AI Evaluation: From Models to Agents

arxiv.org· Filali, Ali El, Bedar, Inès · 2026-05-24 (discovered)

This research paper argues that traditional AI evaluation methodologies, designed for static models and aggregate benchmarks, are inadequate for modern agentic systems. The authors contend that evaluation must evolve from a final checkpoint into a core control function that enables trust and governance rather than serving as performance theater. They identify how standard evaluation pipelines introduce silent failures, benchmark scores mislead development teams, and non-deterministic agent behavior fundamentally changes how performance should be measured. Rather than proposing new metrics, the paper positions evaluation as a measurement discipline essential for iterating on and governing complex, tool-using AI systems operating at scale.

Key takeaways
  • Traditional benchmarks and aggregate scores obscure rather than illuminate agentic system behavior in production
  • Evaluation pipelines themselves introduce silent failure modes that inflate confidence in system reliability
  • High benchmark scores routinely mislead teams about real-world system safety and trustworthiness
  • Agent evaluation must shift from static one-off assessments to continuous control functions enabling governance
  • Non-deterministic system behavior demands fundamentally different performance measurement approaches than model-era practices
#AI evaluation#agentic systems#measurement methodology
Read article →
